title: Ombre Striped Cutting Board
summary: A striped hardwood cutting board built entirely from scrap offcuts arranged in a light-to-dark gradient.
description:
This board exists to use up the offcut bin, not to justify buying new stock. Strips of leftover hardwood in different species and tones are ripped, planed to a common thickness, and glued edge to edge in a graduated light-to-dark order — the result reads as a deliberate ombre effect even though every piece started as scrap.

**Test each strip's color with a dab of finishing oil before you commit to an arrangement.** Raw, unfinished wood can look dramatically lighter or grayer than the same wood does once oiled, and arranging strips by their dry appearance is a common way to end up with a gradient that doesn't actually gradient once the finish goes on.

Because there's no fixed cut list, this plan scales to whatever scrap you have on hand — the only fixed number is the finished 1" thickness every strip needs to share.
timeLabel: 2–3 hrs of shop time plus overnight glue cure

tools.note: Pipe or bar clamps for the full-width glue-up.
Speeds up leveling the glued-up panel before finish sanding.
Cuts a non-rectangular outline, e.g. a teardrop shape.
Drills a hanging hole if you want one.

steps.title: Rip and plane the strips
steps.body: Rip your scrap hardwood into strips of varying width, then plane every strip down to a single consistent thickness of 1" — this is the one number in the whole build that has to be exact, since it becomes the board's finished thickness.

steps.title: Preview colors and arrange the gradient
steps.body: Dab a little finishing oil onto each strip to see its true finished color — raw wood tone is a poor preview of oiled tone. Lay the strips out and arrange them from light to dark until you're happy with the order.

steps.title: Glue up the panel
steps.body: Glue and clamp all the strips edge-to-edge in one glue-up, using bar or pipe clamps to keep even pressure across the full width. Let it cure fully before moving on.

steps.title: Level and sand
steps.body: Scrape off any glue squeeze-out, then sand progressively from a coarse grit up through 220 — a belt sander speeds up the initial leveling pass if the strips aren't perfectly flush.

steps.title: Optional shaping
steps.body: If you want something other than a plain rectangle, trace and cut a shape like a teardrop with a jigsaw, then drill a hanging hole with a forstner bit and round over the edges with a router.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Finish the whole board with food-safe butcher-block oil, reapplying periodically as the board sees use.
